dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 21 at 234 Cliff Street for a sick person.
Hi ma'am, I'm Daniel, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you or someone else call 911 today?
That's me, I'm his sister. He's been in bed for two days-he's got diabetes and cancer, and he fell last week.
One of his toes is black and red, and he hasn't been eating or drinking.
Ma'am, I need to check his responsiveness. Can he answer a few questions for me?
He's awake, but doesn't seem to know the time or the reason we're here.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your full name?
... George. Smith. You know.
What county are we in now?
[STATE]. I think.
Can you tell me what brought you to the hospital today?
I don't know.
Can I look at his eyes with a penlight?
Go ahead. He seems aware of me.
Okay, his pupils are open-responsive to light.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your birthday?
Wrong. It's… something with twenties, I think.
Partner, can we take some vitals?
Ma'am, we're about to take your brother's blood pressure. It won't hurt.
His blood pressure is 194 over 86.
His pulse is 114, strong and regular.
His respiratory rate is 20, unlabored.
Oxygen saturation is at 96%.
Blood glucose level is 342 mg/dL-indicative of hyperglycemia.
Can we get a quick look at his throat and neck?
Of course. He's fine, no signs of blocked airway.
His airway is clear. No signs of obstruction.
His breathing seems normal. Any abnormal colors or sounds in his chest?
He wasn't having much trouble breathing when I saw him.
Yes, respiration is normal and unlabored, no increased work of breathing noted.
I need to examine his skin for condition and color.
His skin is warm, but he is incontinent of urine. No signs of tachypnea.
We'll have to keep an eye on circulation. What medications or conditions are relevant to his medical history?
He has Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, diabetes, and high blood pressure. He's on several medications for those.
Ma'am, I need to ask some more questions to understand what the issue could be. Can you walk me through today's events?
He was fine a week ago until he fell and hurt his left foot, which has been infected and has a black toe since then.
Has he had any other symptoms like pain, dizziness, or fever?
He's had a high temperature the past two days and not eaten anything at all.
Does he have any allergies or medications we should know?
He's got a bee venom allergy, no medication allergies. His meds include Metoprolol, amlodipine, Lyrica, and a few others.
What about his eating and drinking recently?
Just a few sips of water a day. Not enough, obviously.
Any other important background you feel we should be aware of?
He also has had some battles with alcohol too.
Mr. Smith, using a scale from 0 to 10, how would you rate your pain right now?
I don't feel much pain... maybe a 2.
Does anything make the pain worse like movement or rest?
I don't think he mentions pain much.
Partner, can we examine the foot area?
There are red abrasions on the right foot and a dark, excoriated toe on the left-possibly gangrenous.
Starting ECG monitoring now. I'll be running a four-lead setup per protocol.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with a rate of approximately 110 beats per minute.
Initiating intravenous fluids via hyperglycemia protocol.
IV access obtained successfully using an 18-gauge needle. Beginning administration of 750 mL normal saline for hyperglycemia.
We will be following the diabetic hyperglycemia protocol for this case.
Let's check vitals again to assess any change in his condition.
His pulse is now at 120, and respiratory rate is 19.
BP has slightly dropped to 179 over 83.
Glucose reading is now absent, possibly due to prior high readings. Hypoglycemia remains a concern.
Oxygen saturation is 97%, showing slight improvement.
IV fluids are flowing, and the four-lead ECG is in place for continuous monitoring.
Mr. Smith, have you noticed any new or worsening symptoms since we started treatment?
No, not really.
Checking back over the timeline is important in his case. Are there any new developments we need to know?
His fever persists and I worry about the infection but I don't know where it started.
We are tracking signs of sepsis and hyperglycemia, which are notable in this presentation.
Ma'am, will Mr. Smith have a specific hospital in mind for this transport?
I'll trust you in charge-he often goes to [COMPANY_NAME].
We will use a stair chair and transport cot to move him carefully.
Ma'am, everything is ready for a safe lift.
Collected patient identification cards and notes from the plastic grocery bag.
His noted allergies are bee venom. Other meds include Lyrica and hypertensive drugs.
Rechecking vitals-currently stable.
Glasgow Coma Scale is at 14. A&Ox2. Presentation is disheveled, apathetic, and feverish.
Now, his respiratory rate is up to 30, but still no evidence of increased work of breathing.
Elevated respiratory rate noted, but no signs of respiratory distress or failure.
Temperature is currently at 103 degrees Fahrenheit-suggesting fever of possible systemic origin.
Awake and oriented to two out of three. He doesn't seem to react to external stimuli.
Airway remains patent, no swellings or signs of acute airway compromise.
EKG shows consistent sinus tachycardia, stable, with no acute cardiac issues.
There's a documented fall, following foot injury, with evidence of localized infection and sepsis patterns.
IV remains patent and fluids remain in place as planned per protocol.
He's being more disoriented since this morning, not sure if it's related to his infection.
Starting transport via a sheet lift with three persons as reported earlier.
Transport secured to [COMPANY_NAME], scheduled for ED.
Medications and medical records left with the bag as required for hospital staff.
All essential information is compiled and ready to hand off with additional report.
Confirmed all findings and synced EPCR notes with hospital team.
Vitals remain stable and consistent during transport.
IV running as planned and fluids remaining in place.
Still no pain complaints, and condition seems stable enough for continuing transport.
Based on ePCR and family report, likely sepsis with labile hypertension and hyperglycemia involvement.
Temperature remains slightly elevated, with extensive observation ongoing during transport.
Patient's status meets hospital criteria for triage at [COMPANY_NAME].
Transport to [COMPANY_NAME] confirmed and all interventions and protocols in place for arrival.
Nurse alerted upon arrival, regarding his elevated BP and sinus tachycardia for pertinent info.
